title: Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years
subtitle: The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.
caption:
Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years, United States
The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.

Weekly death counts in the United States from January 2020 through early 2021 show distinct deviations from historical baselines and projected expectations. The vertical axis measures the raw number of deaths, ranging from 0 to 100,000, while the horizontal axis tracks time across the year. A black line represents the projected deaths for 2020, derived from reported deaths between 2015 and 2019, hovering consistently between 50,000 and 60,000 throughout the period. Faint grey lines in the background display the actual weekly death counts for those baseline years, clustering tightly around the projection. The colored lines for 2020, 2021, 2022, and 2023 diverge significantly from this baseline. The orange line for 2020 rises above 60,000 in early spring, peaks near 80,000 around April 30, drops back toward the projection in summer, and climbs steeply again in the winter to exceed 80,000 by January 2021. The purple line for 2021 begins the year above 80,000, declines to meet the baseline by spring, and rises again in the autumn to roughly 75,000. The teal line for 2022 and blue line for 2023 follow similar seasonal patterns, dipping near the projection in mid-year and rising to between 60,000 and 70,000 in later months. The data notes that reported figures might not count all deaths due to incomplete coverage and delays in reporting. Across the visible timeline, the years 2020 through 2023 consistently show weekly death counts exceeding the projected baseline during winter and spring periods.
